critic_raw:
The design explicitly states that preprocessing transformers 'are fit on the combined train and validation set before finalizing the model.' In the same paragraph it explains this ensures 'stable estimation.' However, fitting preprocessing parameters (imputation, scaling, TF-IDF vocabulary, feature selection) on both training and validation data and then using the validation set for model selection creates a circular process: the validation set has already influenced the preprocessing parameters that produce the features on which the model is evaluated on the validation set. This is a milder but real form of data leakage. A proper design fits all transformers on training data only.

Section 7 describes a 'final configuration verification on the test set' that 'confirms that hyperparameter tuning was effective' and after which 'test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data.' This is explicit test set contamination. The test set is used to guide training decisions, which means the reported test-set performance is not an unbiased estimate. Using the test set as a secondary validation gate for hyperparameter decisions — regardless of how it is framed — invalidates the test set as an unbiased evaluator.

The auxiliary task label — 'resolution of primary diagnosis marker, medication adherence, functional status' within 14 days post-discharge — is a composite endpoint that is explicitly collected within the follow-up period. This creates a serious temporal overlap concern: if any component of the efficacy label (e.g., functional status) is correlated with early clinical decisions documented in discharge notes, the auxiliary task supervision signal may encode partial readmission risk information that the readmission head then exploits. The design acknowledges that 'missing efficacy labels are retained with sensitivity analysis' but does not address the more fundamental issue of whether the efficacy label itself carries indirect information about readmission risk.

The efficiency hypothesis — that MTL achieves higher AUROC 'with lower model complexity' — confuses two incommensurable dimensions. AUROC measures predictive quality; parameter count and training time measure computational cost. These are not naturally traded off on the same scale. A model that achieves marginally higher AUROC with 50% more parameters might score better on the composite than one that is 50% smaller but matches on AUROC. Without a formal Pareto frontier analysis, claims about 'efficiency' in this combined sense are not well-defined.
defender_raw:
The multi-task learning design for readmission prediction correctly operationalizes the efficiency hypothesis. Testing whether MTL achieves higher AUROC with lower model complexity is a more sophisticated comparison than simply asking whether MTL outperforms single-task — it asks whether the auxiliary task provides free signal, which is the actual theoretical claim behind MTL. This dual criterion (discriminative performance and parameter efficiency) is better aligned with the MTL hypothesis than performance alone.

The treatment efficacy auxiliary task is clinically motivated. Treatment efficacy measured within 14 days post-discharge (resolution of primary diagnosis marker, medication adherence, functional status) is mechanistically related to readmission risk — patients whose treatment is not working are more likely to be readmitted. This is not an arbitrary auxiliary task designed to inject noise; it is a theoretically coherent regularization signal. The composite binary efficacy label is derived from clinical assessments independent of the feedback used in training.

The chronological temporal split (training Jan 2019 - Aug 2022, validation Sept-Dec 2022, test Jan-Dec 2023) reflects the actual deployment scenario: training on historical data, validating on a recent transitional period, and testing on the subsequent year. This is more conservative than stratified random splitting because it includes the temporal distribution shift between training and evaluation.

The baseline — identical architecture with single readmission head — is the correct scientific comparison for MTL. It controls for architecture capacity, training procedure, and data, isolating the contribution of the auxiliary task. Critics who object to the 50,000-patient cohort size overlook that the temporal split leaves roughly 45,000 patients for training, which is sufficient for a shared neural network encoder of the stated size (512→256 units).
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing fitted on combined train+validation set creates circular dependency; validation set statistics influence features evaluated on validation set
Test set is explicitly used to guide training decisions, contaminating the final performance estimate
Post-discharge efficacy composite label may encode indirect readmission risk signal, invalidating the auxiliary task as an independent regularizer
Efficiency hypothesis conflates predictive performance and computational cost metrics that are not naturally comparable
Missing efficacy labels are retained without imputation; their differential availability may systematically bias the training distribution
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing fitted on combined train+validation set creates circular dependency; validation set statistics influence features evaluated on validation set
Test set is explicitly used to guide training decisions, contaminating the final performance estimate
Post-discharge efficacy composite label may encode indirect readmission risk signal, invalidating the auxiliary task as an independent regularizer
Efficiency hypothesis conflates predictive performance and computational cost metrics that are not naturally comparable
